does: This paper introduces RewardHackBench, a benchmark of 13 reward-hacking patterns across high-stakes and general domains, and demonstrates that eight prominent reward models exhibit severe vulnerabilities to specific hacking subcategories. The authors propose HARVE, a training-free method that edits the reward head of scalar reward models by identifying a multi-directional hacking subspace from residual stream directions associated with selected hacking examples, then removes the component of the reward-head vector aligned with this subspace. Using only contrastive gold-hacked pairs without gradient updates or fine-tuning, HARVE improves robustness against reward hacking while preserving the model's general capability across diverse domains.
connects: This work extends recent efforts to evaluate and improve reward model robustness in LLM alignment, building on prior research into reward hacking vulnerabilities and alignment failures. It responds to limitations of fine-tuning approaches by leveraging mechanistic interpretability techniques, specifically residual stream analysis and vector editing methods previously used for other model modifications. The paper connects to the broader safety evaluation literature by providing systematic benchmarks and to interventional alignment work that performs targeted edits to model internals rather than retraining, drawing inspiration from techniques like rank-one model editing and activation-based defenses against adversarial inputs.
enables: This work opens pathways for efficient, scalable defenses against reward hacking that avoid expensive retraining. Future research could extend HARVE to larger models, multi-objective reward setups, and dynamic hacking patterns emerging in deployment. The mechanistic approach suggests directions for understanding and mitigating other alignment failures through residual stream analysis. The RewardHackBench dataset enables further development of detection and mitigation strategies, while the vector-editing framework could inspire similar lightweight interventions for other reward-model vulnerabilities, laying groundwork for more robust LLM alignment without sacrificing training efficiency or general performance.
